text:
RESTAGING BONE SCINTIGRAPHY AND CT ABDOMEN/PELVIS
With Correlative Review of Prior Studies

CLINICAL INDICATION: 69-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC), Gleason 9 (5+4), BRCA2 germline mutation. Currently on enzalutamide 160 mg daily (approximately 6 months of therapy) with ADT continuation. Prior disease course notable for initial response to ADT plus abiraterone followed by confirmed biochemical and radiographic progression at the time of CRPC diagnosis. PSA at most recent check: 1.4 ng/mL (significantly reduced from 8.7 ng/mL baseline at enzalutamide initiation). Restaging requested to assess treatment response in osseous and nodal disease.

---

TECHNIQUE — BONE SCINTIGRAPHY:
Whole-body planar bone scintigraphy was performed approximately 3 hours following intravenous administration of 25 mCi of Technetium-99m methylene diphosphonate (Tc-99m MDP). Anterior and posterior whole-body images obtained. Supplemental SPECT/CT of the pelvis and lumbar spine performed for lesion localization and characterization. Images reviewed in comparison with the prior bone scan obtained at the time of CRPC progression, and the earlier baseline bone scan obtained at the time of initial diagnosis.

TECHNIQUE — CT ABDOMEN/PELVIS:
Multidetector CT of the abdomen and pelvis performed without and with intravenous contrast (100 mL iohexol 350 mg I/mL; rate 3 mL/sec). Given underlying mild chronic kidney disease (baseline creatinine approximately 1.6 mg/dL), IV hydration protocol followed. Portal venous phase acquisitions obtained. Multiplanar reconstructions reviewed. Comparison made to the CT performed at the time of CRPC progression.

Note: CT chest was not performed per ordering provider's request; chest imaging deferred to future evaluation if clinically indicated.

---

FINDINGS — BONE SCINTIGRAPHY:

Overview: When compared to the prior bone scan obtained at CRPC progression (which demonstrated 12 discrete foci of abnormal radiotracer uptake), the current study shows meaningful overall improvement in the degree and extent of osseous tracer activity, consistent with a favorable treatment response in the setting of ongoing enzalutamide therapy.

Spine:
The L2 vertebral body lesion, previously one of the index lesions since initial diagnosis, demonstrates persistent but noticeably diminished radiotracer uptake compared to the prior study. The T9 vertebral body lesion similarly shows reduced tracer activity with no expansion of the involved area. The T12 vertebral body lesion, which was newly identified at CRPC progression, now shows substantially decreased uptake, consistent with meaningful partial response at this site. No new vertebral lesions are identified. No abnormal uptake is seen in the cervical spine, thoracic spine above T9, or lumbar spine below L2 that would suggest new involvement.

Pelvis and Hip Region:
The left iliac wing lesion and right acetabular lesion, previously showing increased tracer uptake at the time of CRPC progression after an interval of relative improvement on abiraterone, again demonstrate decreased uptake compared to that most recent prior scan. The degree of uptake at these sites approaches or is somewhat below what was seen on the initial staging bone scan, reflecting continued treatment benefit.

The left femoral head lesion, which was a new finding at the time of CRPC progression, now shows partial response with reduced radiotracer accumulation. The lesion is still perceptible above background, but is considerably less prominent than at the prior study.

The right femoral neck lesion, while not showing definitive interval improvement, appears overall stable in extent and intensity of tracer uptake when compared to the prior scan. No progression at this site.

Ribs and Thoracic Cage:
The previously identified lesions in the right 4th rib and left 6th and 7th ribs, which had largely responded to prior abiraterone therapy and showed near-resolution on the restaging scan after initial ADT+abiraterone, remain photopenic to near-normal in appearance. No convincing residual abnormal tracer uptake at these sites. No new rib lesions identified.

Humerus, Clavicles, Sternum, Skull:
No abnormal foci of radiotracer accumulation identified in the bilateral upper extremities, sternum, clavicles, or skull.

Summary — Bone Scan: No new osseous lesions are identified on the current study. The overall burden of osseous tracer activity has decreased compared to the prior scan performed at the time of CRPC progression. Specific lesions demonstrating partial response include the T12 vertebral body and left femoral head. Lesions at the L2 and T9 vertebral bodies, left iliac wing, and right acetabulum show persistent but reduced activity. The right femoral neck lesion is stable. This pattern is consistent with a favorable, though incomplete, osseous response to enzalutamide.

---

FINDINGS — CT ABDOMEN/PELVIS:

Liver and Biliary System:
The liver is normal in size and demonstrates homogeneous parenchymal attenuation in the portal venous phase without focal hepatic lesions identified. No hypodense or hyperdense focal abnormalities. No biliary ductal dilatation. Gallbladder is present without stones or wall thickening. Portal and hepatic veins are patent.

Spleen:
Mildly enlarged at approximately 13.2 cm in craniocaudal dimension, likely reflective of chronic disease and/or portal hemodynamics. No focal splenic lesions.

Pancreas:
Pancreas is unremarkable with normal attenuation throughout the head, body, and tail. No pancreatic ductal dilatation. No peripancreatic inflammatory changes. No focal mass.

Adrenal Glands:
Bilateral adrenal glands appear within normal limits. No adrenal nodules or enlargement identified. Given prior abiraterone therapy, adrenal morphology is specifically noted as unremarkable.

Kidneys and Ureters:
Bilateral kidneys demonstrate slightly reduced cortical thickness consistent with the known history of mild chronic kidney disease. No hydronephrosis. No obstructing calculi. No renal masses. Bilateral small cortical cysts, largest measuring approximately 9 mm in the left kidney, unchanged and consistent with benign simple cysts. No perinephric fluid collections.

Bladder and Prostate:
The urinary bladder contains a moderate volume of urine. No bladder wall irregularity or intraluminal mass identified. The prostate gland region demonstrates post-treatment changes with overall stable appearance. No new pelvic soft tissue masses are seen adjacent to the prostate bed.

Bowel:
Small and large bowel loops are unremarkable in caliber and wall thickness. No bowel obstruction. No pneumatosis or free air. No appendiceal abnormality.

Peritoneum and Retroperitoneum:
No ascites. No peritoneal nodularity or omental thickening to suggest peritoneal carcinomatosis.

Lymph Nodes:
Comparison is made to the prior CT at CRPC progression, at which time the right obturator node measured 1.6 cm, the left common iliac node measured 1.2 cm, and new right para-aortic and aortocaval nodes were identified measuring up to 1.4 cm and 1.1 cm, respectively.

On the current study:
- Right obturator lymph node: now measuring approximately 0.9 cm in short axis, representing a clear interval decrease from the prior 1.6 cm measurement.
- Left common iliac lymph node: now approximately 0.8 cm (previously 1.2 cm), reduced in size.
- Right para-aortic lymph node: approximately 0.9 cm (previously 1.4 cm), decreased in size.
- Aortocaval lymph node: approximately 0.8 cm (previously 1.1 cm), decreased in size and now approaching normal upper limit.
- Left external iliac lymph node region: no definitively enlarged nodes identified; previously noted node at 0.6 cm (from abiraterone restaging) not convincingly re-enlarged.
- No new pelvic, inguinal, retroperitoneal, or mesenteric lymph nodes meeting criteria for enlargement.

Overall, there is interval regression of pelvic and retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy compared to the prior scan, consistent with partial nodal treatment response.

Bone Windows (CT):
The osseous structures visible on the CT confirm the findings reported on the bone scan. Sclerotic changes are present in the L2 and T9 vertebral bodies, left iliac wing, and right acetabulum, consistent with previously described bone metastases. No new lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions are identified. The right femoral neck demonstrates a small area of cortical sclerosis at the known metastatic site, stable in appearance without cortical disruption or endosteal scalloping to suggest impending pathologic fracture at this time.

Aorta and Major Vessels:
The aorta is mildly ectatic in caliber, consistent with age. No aneurysmal dilatation. Inferior vena cava is patent. No filling defects suggestive of thrombosis.

Soft Tissues:
No subcutaneous masses or edematous changes of clinical significance. Mild lower extremity soft tissue edema is noted, consistent with the known clinical history.

Lung Bases (limited evaluation):
The imaged lung bases on CT are visualized without definitive nodular abnormalities in the regions sampled. Note that full thoracic evaluation was not performed on this study.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Overall favorable response to enzalutamide in the setting of mCRPC, with improvement seen across both osseous and nodal disease compartments compared to the prior restaging study performed at CRPC progression.

2. Bone scintigraphy demonstrates reduction in radiotracer uptake at multiple sites of previously documented osseous metastatic disease. Notable partial response at the T12 vertebral body and left femoral head lesions. Remaining lesions, including L2, T9, left iliac wing, and right acetabulum, show decreased but residual abnormal uptake. The right femoral neck lesion is stable without clear response or progression. No new osseous lesions identified.

3. CT abdomen/pelvis demonstrates regression of pelvic and retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. The right obturator node has decreased from 1.6 cm to 0.9 cm, the left common iliac node from 1.2 cm to 0.8 cm, and the para-aortic and aortocaval nodes have similarly diminished.

4. No hepatic, adrenal, or other visceral metastatic lesions identified on the current study.

5. No evidence of bowel obstruction, hydronephrosis, or other acute abdominal/pelvic pathology.

6. Stable sclerotic osseous changes at known metastatic sites on CT without new lytic lesions or imaging features to suggest impending pathologic fracture.

Residual osseous and nodal metastatic disease is present. Continued clinical and imaging surveillance is appropriate in the context of ongoing systemic therapy.
